speaker: interviewer
text: One, Michael Brand.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] It is December 21, 1995. Testimony will be given by Dr. Michael Brand. My name is Renee Firestone. We are in Ventura, California in Los-- in-- in the United States. And the language will be English.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is December 21, 1995. Testimony will be given by Dr. Michael Brand, M-I-C-H-A-E-L capital B-R-A-N-D. My name is Renee Firestone, R-E-N-E-E F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. We are in Ventura, California in the United States. And the language will be English. Good morning, Dr. Brand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for permitting us to come and speak to you this morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, would you please state your name? Tell us when and where were you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Michael Brand. The last name spelled B-R-A-N-D. I was born in the small town by the name Radziwellow, R-A-D-Z-I-W-E-L-L-O-W, Radziwellow. I was born the 11th of February, 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: Radziwellow was where? What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia. It-- it was Russia when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what your father's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Salamon Brandwein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about him. What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very wonderful man. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was very devoted to the family. I had a sister. And my mother was a-- a socially-minded person. She was by profession a midwife and an assistant doctor. And we-- we spent our time with my father talking, going walking, sports. And in general, it was a wonderful family, and I have only pleasant memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you lost your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my mother was killed the 20th of May, 1943. I don't know, unfortunately, the birthday of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your-- the name of your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rachel, [? Raichel ?] in Russian, Rachel Brandwein. And she-- I don't remember her date of birth. But she was born in Radziwellow. All my family comes from Radziwellow. Most of my family comes from Radziwellow, a town by 5,000 or 7,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was looking for the children, and she was the boss in the family. She told me, you have to study. If you don't study, you'll be a nothing. And then when I graduate from high school, she said, you'll be a doctor. And I have no-- didn't have no-- no answer to that. I became a doctor because my mother told me so. And I enjoyed every minute of medical studies in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, you had any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a sister by the name Gina, G-I-N-A, in-- in Hebrew, Hinda, H-I-N-D-A. She was-- she was born three years after I was born, in 1915. And she was a very good student, specializing in mathematics in high school. Unfortunately, she died of toxic myocarditis following diphtheria in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she when she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was, 1915, 15 years old-- 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of that time when she passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly high school, mostly high school, high school school work. I played soccer, and we had friends. There was not much money to go gallivanting, you know. And getting together and a lot of reading because it didn't cost any money. So we had two libraries in my hometown. And everybody was reading because there was no money for any expensive entertainment or recreation, so forth. Once in a while, we went on picnics together with other friends of my parents, you know, and also occasional movies. You know, but mostly it was home, getting together and talking of interesting current actual events. And Israel was a very important subject of conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were Zionists. My father was a Zionist, and most of the people, naturally, living in Poland, which was really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not very conducive to feel comfortable and free. Israel was a very important part of our life-- in the future Israel, I should say. We worked for-- we collected money. There was a pushke, a blue box with donations. Every Friday, when my mother said blessings on the candles, she put in money.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back for a moment. I want  you to recall the day when your sister passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. It was terrible. My mother was-- became white like a pigeon overnight. She was sick about three-- two, three weeks. That's all. Because she caught pneumonia and then affected her sick heart. And she died in-- in bed at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you around while she was sick? Were you around?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was around. Sure, I was home. I was in high-- I graduated in high school the year-- from high school-- I graduated from high school 1930, the year my sister died. And she five minutes-- 10 minutes before she died, my mother ordered, ordered, a neighbor to get a professional photographer, and he came and took pictures. And I'm sure there's somewhere a picture five minutes before my sister died. She sat up, and she asked for a mirror to straighten out her hair. She had beautiful blonde hair. And-- and just lie down and die, and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, give me a mirror, and she fix-- fixed  her hair. And she couldn't talk. Then she lied down straight. Very beautiful girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether your friends came   to the funeral?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends, no. Because they were boy-- you know, we were 17, 18-year-old. But we had a big funeral, big funeral. And a big matzevah-- my   mother ordered a big, tall, modern, very elegant. Then the Germans used the matzevah like any other monuments of the Jewish cemetery for-- for-- to-- for-- for sidewalks, for sidewalks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether her  friends came to  the funeral?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't remember, but I don't think so. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't customary for the young boys or girls, high school boys, to go to funerals. It wasn't, you know. It wasn't customary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether you had non-Jewish friends at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very many, no. My-- you know, in Poland, we had some non-Jewish friends. But mostly they were interested in helping-- in-- in solving their problems, especially school, comparing-- pertaining to school work. Because the Jewish students worked harder and knew more than non-Jewish student-- students. And everybody has two,  three friends, which are needed only when-- when they needed help. But otherwise, no social activity, no social contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to elementary school where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radziwellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- do you remember your teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember the-- the director-- it was a Polish school-- and the director. And then I remember the geography teacher. He was a cripple. And then we had a Hebrew teacher, religion-- a religion-- a Hebrew teacher, Mr. Grockstein. He was a little funny once in a while, acted funny, because we are not altar boys, maybe including me maybe. I don't remember. He said we're unruly. He had funny [INAUDIBLE] customs, like standing in the corner. He was carrying peas, hard peas, in his pocket, to put  in the peas on the floor by-- in the corner. And you had to stand on your knees, on your knees, was your-- you knees-- with your knees on the peas. [LAUGHS] And he was-- but he was-- that's all right. He was-- and we learned something, not very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage of   the student body do you think was Jewish in this Polish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In elementary school? 20%, 20%.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: High school Brody, B-R-O-D-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was no high school in Radziwellow. So we moved to Brody for my sake and my sister's sake, because there was a-- a school for girls, a high school for girls. And my-- my high school was a government school. We are mixed, interdenominational, and there were boys and girls. But eventually, they made it just strictly for boys, for male students. And in fact, our class was the last one. We had four girls in-- in the class. After that, just only boys were admitted to the-- to the high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any teachers in high school that you remembered for any particular reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were some Jewish professors. There was Professor Berenhaut who was a friend of an uncle of mine. And I remember before I would be admitted to the-- applying for admission to the high school, our  uncle, who was a friend of Dr. Berenhaut, of Professor Berenhaut we visited in his home, and he got acquainted with me. He probably had something to do with that. And there was a Professor [? Buis, ?] who was teaching Latin. We had Latin four years. And a Professor Zeinfeld for math and a Professor Konfer, all Jews, teaching physics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was high school available for all Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, mostly. There was a little subterfuge. We went to the director, and he said, would you make a donation to the school orchestra? So my mother took out 100 zloty. That was a lot of money. It was $25. And she gave it to him, and he put  it in his pocket. But that's OK. I didn't mind. But that was supposed to be allegedly for the benefit of the school orchestra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any anti-Semitism in the high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not active anti-Semitism because socially we didn't have any contact. In the intermission, the-- in between classes, the boys, the Jewish-- Jewish students, stuck together-- spend their time together eating their sandwich. And the Polish or the Ukrainian separate. You know, there was no social contact. But they-- like I said, in-- in case of need, they asked their so-called Jewish friends to help them out-- math, physics, Latin--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you said you had Polish and Ukrainian population--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brody.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Brody. Let me ask you, did you know this, or do you know whether there was a difference in attitude of the Polish students and the Polish people and the-- and the Ukrainian people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think. I didn't analyze it. You know, I was in-- we had a lot of homework. We went to school six days a week. We had only two weeks vacation for Christmas and two-month vacation for summer. And everybody was busy with his work and borrowing books because nobody could afford all the books. We were interchanging school books between-- with the other students. But there was no-- I didn't any difference. You know. But the principle was that, stick to your own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you finish high school?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to Prague. I-- I didn't even apply to a medical school in Poland, because there was a numerus clausus. Numerus clausus means there was a limited-- either closed for Jews or if you have 5,000-- if the father had a lot of money, which my father didn't, 5,000 and up, and you know the appropriate person, possibly, you could get in. In my-- in Brody, there was one Jewish student who was admitted to medical school in Poland. But his father was the secretary of the court, Jewish fellow, and they had connections, so to say. And that's what happened. He  was admitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, in Prague, medical school free for everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. For-- for-- first of all, they admit everybody who had the qualifications and high school education, and then, the-- the immigrant, the-- the foreign student, what bringing money to the country, and whatever they brought. But they were-- there was a certain-- you know, for business reasons. They admitted these students, from-- mostly Jewish students from all the countries, Hungary, Poland, Bulgaria, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Greece, and [INAUDIBLE] Ukraine-- from Ukraine, too. But I mean, they were more liberal. There were two medical schools. There was a Czech-- Czech medical school and a German medical school. And both schools were known, because my school was founded in the 15th century and had a tradition. And they had a excellent upstanding, outstanding teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the German medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. German Medical School of Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the student-- Jewish student body, was the Jewish student body organized in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we had a lot of Zionist activities, and the corporations under German style, so-called fighting corporations. And of course, there was-- not everybody belonged to a corporation. I belonged, you know, just to be a-- have a group of people that are close. We met every Saturday. We had one beer, and we were talking about Zionist problems, and had speakers. And once in a while, dances with the Jewish-- we invited just Jewish girls. And it was a social life in-- in-- in a limited way, because everybody worked very hard. Saturday. Saturday evening, that's the time to have-- we had-- everybody had a girlfriend. Not-- many-- many boys had a girlfriend. I had a girlfriend. And there was there, in Prague, the Jewish life was concentrated in a Jewish center, Cafe Ash, A-S-H, in the old part of-- of Prague. The historical part of Prague. And was once a complex, and in the basement was a big movie, [? Roxie, ?] I remember. There was a big cafe and a restaurant, and rooms for functions-- for functions, where all kind of organizations, including our corporation, we just made a reservation. And we had to order one beer. Nobody could-- not everybody could afford more than one beer, because it was every Saturday. It wasn't custom. It was not compulsory. And then we talked. We had, always, a speaker, you know, [? Jean ?] [? Baptiste. ?] He was, at that time, the number one speaker, I remember. And we had exercises with swords, like the German corporations, but it was just, you know, to keep up the tradition of the fighting corporation. And we were singing Zionist, Jewish songs, and German songs, Polish songs. We had, also, students from Latvia, and Lithuania, Estonia, and [INAUDIBLE] and Lithuanian, yeah. Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia. And-- and so, everybody-- there were some students who were living on $15 a month, like me, and some students lived on $10 a month. At that time, it was $15 was middle class, $10 was poor. And some-- maybe some students, who were very few, had more money. But the more money they had, the less they did studying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who financed your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you finish medical school?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home. I went to Brody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I had a foreign diploma. I couldn't practice medicine in-- in Poland. To-- to practice medicine, you have to be admitted for nostrification. That means you have to take a test. All the material in Polish-- in Polish-- Polish. And then they give you it. But I couldn't have dreamed even to be admitted to nostrification.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was a Jew. And my father didn't have any, and it wasn't worth $5,000 or $10,000. He couldn't afford it. There was one, like I told you before, this one student, was in-- graduated Prague. He was a nostrification, Schwartz, I remember. Because his father was a secretary of the court, and he had connections. Anyway, he was the only one. And there was another Jewish student who-- who became Christian, converted to Christianity, and then get they admitted him to medical school in Krakow. He converted. His-- his father was the director of the [INAUDIBLE]. It was the health department-- health department. Like here, you know, HMO or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I had a friend who went to high school with  me. And he was a very capable newspaper man. He graduate from high school. His father was a-- not a shoemaker, a-- a-- a watchmaker. He didn't have any money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to Warsaw on a lark. It was about 400 miles. He left for the town, because Brody didn't have any prospects. When I went to medical school, he went to Warsaw, 1930. And [? he leave ?] [? '31. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a Jewish newspaper in Polish, [? Naz ?] [? Shaglun. ?] He understand a little bit Polish. And he became their-- start to work from the bottom. And eventually started-- he was in the administration, and the circulation department. Eventually, he became a newspaper man, writing articles. In-- in-- in the-- in high school, he was known for poems. He wrote poetry-- poetry. And he was raised, practically, in our home. I don't think it's relevant, but his father married the second-- his second wife, didn't like him. He was-- so he grew-- he was coming to our home, and my mother was his, you know, food, wash out the shirt. He wasn't very clean, because his mother-- his-- his stepmother didn't want him around, even. And he was a good soccer player, and so forth. Anyway, when I came to Prague-- with the diploma, which didn't mean nothing, couldn't make a living, I wrote him a letter. He lived in Warsaw. So I told him the situation. He said, come over to Warsaw. Come over to Warsaw. And my mother went to a resort, because she had a rheumatism, arthritis. I was by myself, and my father was on a business trip. And there was a truck with eggs parked in front of our-- in the area where we lived. I came to the chauffeur and asked if I come-- can go with him to Prague. It's about 400 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: He say, sure, hop in. So I closed-- I closed the apartments, took a little suitcase with a few shirts, and I rode straight to-- to Warsaw. I got his address. And-- we started-- he came from a-- he was a-- every year, every week, he wanted to get married to another girl, you know? He was a soft-hearted guy, very good man, very good boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, he was away. I sit on the porch, on the steps for about two hours. About 10 o'clock at night, he showed up. And he said, OK, fine, after we were kissing, and shaking, and this. [SIGHS] And I started to work in the Jewish hospital in Warsaw, without pay. I was like a-- a intern. The big hospital in-- in Warsaw. They're strictly Jewish. All the-- all the-- all the doctors were Jewish, and so, it was called the Jewish Hospital. I had to make a few dollars, so I talked to the doctor who was the head of the Department of Internal Medicine. And so, he sent me out to give shots to his students at home and traveling on street cars. And net, I made 20 cents a-- a shot, 20 cents. And anyway, it was-- we had the wonderful time. We had a wonderful-- we had a wonderful time. Boys, no problems. Nobody to support. Just get a-- a meal and so forth. And then I got a regular job in a former SS hospital in Augsburg. I-- I was-- I don't remember how I got the connection, but somebody recommended me. In Augsburg, there was only for SS. Sick SS people, the Gestapo, before the war, during the German time period. And I got there, a job, a job with-- with a private room, with three meals served in the room. I was a doctor. I had-- and I was in charge of a-- one group of-- they had certain amount of beds, you know, [INAUDIBLE] beds, was my responsibility. And there were patients from six, seven, 15, 10 nationalities, because they were all in concentration camps or in work camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1938 and 1939. No. It was 1937, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Germans occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. No, I'm sorry. I have to backtrack. I'm sorry. It's completely different. I'm saying in Warsaw, I talked to this doctor who was in charge, Dr. Landsberg. He says there is that private hospital for tuberculosis in [? Oetwurtsk. ?] That was about 20 miles from-- from Warsaw. They need a house physician, and talk to Mrs. Shagoda. Dr. Shagoda was a Jewish fellow who was a-- the owner of this hospital. He died. And Mrs. Shagoda his wife, was in charge of the hospital. There was a head doctor that was engaged head doctor, and we had two more doctors-- Jewish doctors from Poland. And I worked there in this hospital, also the same thing. I had a private room, three meals, laundry, everything, comfortable. Until-- till-- until the war broke out, September, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, in Warsaw, while you were performing medical work--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: With non-Jews, no. No, at all. Not at all. Not at all, because I was, again, in the beginning, I started night duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked there every night, every night. And then, I worked during the day. And when I came home, my-- my friend, David, he says, what are you sleeping? I said, because I'm tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, let's go. I got nice-- we have two sisters. You go with one, and I'll go with the other one. Said, no, I'm not interested. He say, I hold him back, because he was very sentimental.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a good boy, but he worked very hard. He wrote articles, and also was involved in circulation of the paper, [? Naz ?] [? Shaglun, ?] I remember. Did you have-- did you have any knowledge of any anti-Semitism in-- in Warsaw at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no contact with Polish whatsoever, whatsoever. My-- my people that I met were Jews. The hospital was Jewish. All the patients were Jewish. And the doctors were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will stop--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will stop for a moment. We're going to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll [? talk to you. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not--
